responses.strategic_rationale: The project confronts the tension between rigorous longitudinal data collection and the practical realities of a residential research unit integrated into a care setting. Core trade-offs include maximizing physiological data quality while preserving participant safety and comfort, protecting privacy in a highly observable environment, and balancing pilot feasibility with scalable impact. The chosen levers address tiered sensing to optimize data yield without overburdening participants, governance and data stewardship to enable future reuse under GDPR, staffing models that ensure safe coverage across eight suites, recruitment strategies to hit enrollment targets, and a staged expansion framework linked to demonstrated data quality and event capture. Together, they aim to de-risk a 3-year plan while preserving scientific integrity.

levers.name: Residential capture workflow optimization
levers.consequences: Establishing a tiered sensing stack and escalation pathway increases the likelihood of capturing parasomnia events while limiting intrusiveness on initial nights. The downstream implications include added data integration challenges across modalities and potential delays in escalation when clinical events occur, which may affect data completeness for certain participants.
options: Adopt tiered nightly sensing with predominantly dry-electrode EEG and contact-free sensors in the initial nights, reserving full PSG only on escalation nights to maximize data yield while minimizing intrusion.
Reinstate a fixed cadence of enhanced nights (nights 1, mid, final) to standardize scoring across participants, improving comparability even if some nights yield redundant data.
Implement an adaptive escalation protocol that triggers full PSG after technician flags events, balancing clinical capture with staffing efficiency and reducing unnecessary overnight measurements.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances data richness with participant burden and safety, but the options overlook contingencies for equipment downtime or data loss and do not address quality control during extended stays.

levers.name: Event triage and annotation pipeline governance
levers.consequences: Standardized real-time annotations will accelerate downstream scoring and improve inter-rater reliability. The downstream implications include increased training demands for night staff and potential bottlenecks if adjudication capacity is insufficient.
options: Standardize real-time annotations by trained night technicians using a concise event tagging scheme to speed scoring and improve inter-rater reliability.
Pilot double-blind independent scoring with a fixed adjudication protocol to quantify agreement and detect systematic biases in event morphology.
Invest in semi-automation of triage with a human-in-the-loop ranking to prioritize probable parasomnia episodes while preserving clinician oversight.
levers.review_lever: Data handling and privacy are central here, but the options miss explicit strategies for consent renewal and governance of long-term data reuse or international sharing.

levers.name: Data governance, privacy, and repository strategy
levers.consequences: Strict de-identification and access controls protect participant privacy and support responsible data reuse. The downstream implications include potential delays in data access for external researchers and the need for formalized data-use agreements to avoid scope creep.
options: Implement strict de-identification and access controls prior to deposition to a recognized repository, ensuring privacy while enabling secondary analyses under data use agreements.
Define a tiered data access model that allows researchers to request restricted datasets with approved research plans and data-use agreements, balancing transparency with privacy constraints.
Regularly audit data lifecycles, encryption, and backups to maintain compliance with university policies and GDPR, while ensuring robust recoverability after incidents.
levers.review_lever: Privacy controls are essential, but the options overlook mechanisms to facilitate timely secondary analyses while maintaining compliance and clear consent parameters.

levers.name: Staffing model and shift pattern optimization
levers.consequences: Shifting to extended hours with cross-trained technicians can maintain coverage across eight suites while preserving safety margins, but increases fatigue risk and labor costs; robust scheduling and rest periods are needed.
options: Adopt rotating 12-hour night shifts with cross-trained technicians to maintain continuous coverage across eight active suites while preserving safety margins.
Pilot a dedicated on-call physician liaison during escalation nights to manage clinical events without compromising research workflows.
Implement a staggered onboarding plan with mentorship from senior staff to sustain scoring consistency and reduce turnover during the pilot.
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses staffing safety and coverage, but the options miss a robust contingency plan for illness, burnout, or turnover that would affect nights.

levers.name: Recruitment, retention, and feasibility governance
levers.consequences: Structured pre-admission screening improves eligibility estimates and reduces unproductive admissions, boosting yield; however, stricter criteria may slow enrollment and risk sample diversity.
options: Strengthen the pre-admission pathway with structured clinical interviews and collateral history to improve eligibility estimation and reduce unproductive admissions.
Incentivize retention through family involvement and transparent scheduling while maintaining strict ethical standards and avoiding coercive practices.
Introduce quarterly enrollment reviews and adaptive criteria adjustments to respond to interim event yield and safety metrics.
levers.review_lever: NAVIATIONNOTE: This lever navigates recruitment and retention, but the options overlook strategies to preserve sample diversity and address consent tensions with bed partner participation.

levers.name: Expansion timing, pilot proof points, and governance triggers
levers.consequences: Defining milestones for expansion ties scale to demonstrated data quality, reducing waste and risk; however, strict go/no-go criteria may slow progress if early results require longer validation.
options: Define explicit milestones for expansion to a larger facility only after achieving predefined event capture and data quality benchmarks aligned to Aim 2.
Establish a staged governance protocol with transparent go/no-go criteria and external review triggers at month 18 or earlier if risk signals emerge.
Proactively plan for external funding diversification and potential pharmaceutical partnerships with predefined publication rights to safeguard research integrity.
levers.review_lever: This lever binds expansion to predefined metrics, but the options miss a clear post-expansion evaluation framework and exit criteria if data quality declines.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia program sits at the intersection of methodological ambition and operational practicality. Core tensions include data fidelity versus safety and comfort, regulatory and privacy constraints, upfront capital versus long-term research payoffs, and the need for scalable workflows as the cohort grows. The levers target: (1) optimizing sensing and escalation for high-quality event capture, (2) robust data governance for reproducibility and compliance, (3) staffing and safety models that sustain coverage without unsustainable costs, (4) diverse recruitment to improve generalizability, (5) efficient adjudication to enable timely publications, and (6) scalable storage to support long-term, multi-year analyses. Together they balance speed, quality, cost, and ethics to maximize scientific return.

levers.name: Sensing modality prioritization and escalation logic gating
levers.consequences: Shifting emphasis toward high-fidelity overnight PSG on flagged nights improves diagnostic precision and reduces false positives, enabling clearer event characterization. The downstream trade-off is higher staffing demand during escalation periods and potential bottlenecks for routine nights if resources are constrained.
options: Adopt a tiered escalation protocol that activates full PSG only after validated caregiver flags and corroborating sensor patterns
Standardize a fixed nightly low-burden monitoring with dry-electrode EEG and contactless sensors, reserving higher-complexity PSG for mid-stay nights
Develop adaptive escalation rules based on real-time data quality metrics to optimize resource use without compromising safety
levers.review_lever: This lever balances resource intensity against diagnostic fidelity, but the options miss scalable staffing models for peak occupancy and a clear plan for long-run cost implications.

levers.name: Longitudinal data governance and curation cadence
levers.consequences: Instituting rigorous data lifecycle management and consistent metadata practices improves reproducibility and regulatory compliance for multi-year studies. However, overly rigid processes may slow iterative analyses and reduce agility in protocol adjustments.
options: Implement a tiered data access model with role-based permissions and regular audits to protect privacy while enabling timely research collaborations
Institute automated metadata capture and EEG-BIDS validation pipelines to accelerate scoring and downstream analyses while maintaining data quality
Define quarterly governance reviews with external auditors and ethics oversight to align with evolving regulations and funder requirements
levers.review_lever: This lever embeds data stewardship into operations, but the options overlook practical thresholds for approval velocity and a lightweight framework for external data sharing.

levers.name: Night-shift capacity optimization and safety staffing framework
levers.consequences: Reconfiguring shift patterns improves coverage during high-need nights and reduces fatigue risks for staff, supporting data integrity. The trade-off is higher personnel costs and potential scheduling complexity that could affect participant recruitment and retention.
options: Establish cross-disciplinary cross-training and flexible on-call rosters to ensure safe coverage during peak activity nights
Adopt staggered shift blocks and fatigue-awareness protocols to maintain alertness and data quality across extended monitoring periods
Contract part-time specialists from partner departments to scale response during escalation nights without permanent headcount expansion
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses staffing scalability, but the options miss proactive retention strategies and potential cross-site training bottlenecks.

levers.name: Participant recruitment pathway diversification
levers.consequences: Diversifying recruitment channels increases the pool of eligible participants and improves cohort representativeness, but it introduces additional consent workflows and local regulatory considerations.
options: Forge regional partnerships with regional sleep clinics to funnel eligible participants through standardized pre-admission pathways
Develop targeted outreach campaigns for underrepresented communities to improve cohort diversity while upholding rigorous consent processes
Implement a flexible compensation model aligned with local living costs to reduce withdrawal risk and maintain enrollment momentum
levers.review_lever: This lever expands recruitment reach, but options overlook the operational cost implications and consent burden variance across sites.

levers.name: Event adjudication efficiency through calibration and workflow redesign
levers.consequences: Calibrating scorers and clarifying workflows reduces adjudication time and improves inter-rater reliability; downstream, it may constrain nuanced judgments for borderline cases if criteria become too rigid.
options: Institute quarterly scorer calibration sessions with predefined consensus criteria and independent adjudication check-ins to harmonize judgments
Implement a tiered scoring protocol that routes borderline cases to a senior scorer for final adjudication
Adopt a standardized annotation template to streamline review flow while preserving essential descriptive detail across cases
levers.review_lever: This lever can speed adjudication, but options miss strategies to handle rare edge cases that require nuanced interpretation.

levers.name: Technology and platform scalability for longitudinal data storage
levers.consequences: Planning scalable storage and robust backups protects data integrity for a growing cohort and long-term studies, enabling future analyses and sharing; but it requires upfront architecture investment and ongoing maintenance.
options: Consolidate EEG-BIDS compatible local NAS with encrypted backups and defined retention windows to support reproducibility and regulatory compliance
Negotiate cloud-enabled transfer and selective replication policies with university storage to balance cost, latency, and accessibility
Institute a quarterly storage health audit and data integrity checks to detect silent corruption and ensure long-term dataset availability
levers.review_lever: This lever secures data longevity, but options overlook cost controls and potential regulatory constraints on cloud data.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia program sits at the intersection of safety, ecological validity, data quality, participant burden, privacy, and scalable governance. The six levers target: (1) aligning the sleep environment with naturalistic behavior without compromising safety, (2) calibrating sensing fidelity to balance data richness and operational workload, (3) refining recruitment and pre-screening workflows to sustain enrollment, (4) strengthening data governance and annotation practices for reproducibility, (5) establishing a semi-automated event triage workflow with rigorous human adjudication, and (6) instituting stage-gate capacity expansion to grow responsibly. This combination navigates early-stage feasibility while preserving scientific rigor and publishable outputs, without prematurely committing to large-scale expansion or unproven autonomous tooling.

levers.name: Naturalistic sleep environment fidelity and safety balance
levers.consequences: Direct effect: improves ecological validity by reducing artificial constraints on sleep behavior; downstream implication: safety monitoring becomes more intrusive and resource-intensive, with increased staffing needs and higher operational costs. The broader observational load also necessitates enhanced data handling workflows and privacy safeguards.
options: Reconfigure room ambience and safety measures to maximize naturalistic sleep behavior while preserving edge alarms and fire safety.
Implement adaptive safety interventions that escalate only on objective risk indicators, reducing observer intrusion during stable nights.
Schedule staff presence to minimize resident disturbance during critical sleep windows while maintaining rapid response capability.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: safety requirements versus naturalistic sleep observation, but the options overlook long-term maintenance costs and scheduling rigidity.

levers.name: Tiered sensing fidelity management across study timeline
levers.consequences: Direct effect: reduces participant burden while preserving data integrity; downstream: risk of missing rare events if quality gates are too strict, potentially slowing study progression. Also increases governance overhead for ongoing threshold tuning and cross-night harmonization.
options: Adopt a phased data collection plan that prioritizes dry-electrode headbands early and expands to full PSG on clinically flagged nights, reducing participant burden while preserving diagnostic yield.
Introduce a dynamic data quality gate that delays full PSG deployment until interim inter-rater reliability meets predefined thresholds.
Establish a centralized calibration protocol for sensor drift and environmental noise across all nights to maintain data comparability.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: data completeness versus throughput; weakness: the options miss explicit strategies for balancing sensitivity and specificity in gating and handling missed events.

levers.name: Collateral-informed recruitment and screening optimization
levers.consequences: Direct effect: improves screen-to-enrollment efficiency and sample representativeness; downstream: increases upfront administrative workload and potential for selection bias if collateral data evolves over time.
options: Integrate collateral histories from bed partners and prior PSG reports into automated eligibility scoring to improve enrollment efficiency.
Pilot regionally targeted outreach through clinician referrals and campus screening days to raise enrollment yield without overburdening staff.
Introduce staged consent and pre-screening questionnaires to reduce dropouts before scheduling in Bonn and ensure eligibility alignment with study criteria.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: recruitment speed versus screening rigor; weakness: options miss potential ethical concerns or partner burnout.

levers.name: EEG-BIDS data governance and annotation cadence
levers.consequences: Direct effect: improved reliability and regulatory compliance for longitudinal data; downstream: increased governance overhead and documentation burden that may impact study velocity.
options: Institute a double-scoring workflow with independent raters and a fixed adjudication protocol to improve reliability.
Implement encrypted nightly backups and strict access controls to meet regulatory and privacy requirements across all data streams.
Standardize a shared data dictionary and audit trail to enable reproducible analyses and cross-study comparisons.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: reliability versus governance overhead; weakness: options miss how to handle versioning across annotation releases or cross-site interoperability.

levers.name: Semi-automated event triage workflow with human adjudication
levers.consequences: Direct effect: reduces manual workload and improves consistency in event adjudication; downstream: rigid criteria may miss nuanced events, potentially delaying rare episode confirmation.
options: Develop a rule-based ranking system that flags high-likelihood events for immediate human review while routing routine nights for batch scoring.
Define clear scoring criteria and thresholds to minimize ambiguous cases and maintain high inter-rater agreement across independent scorers.
Create a documentation protocol to trace decision paths and support reproducibility in longitudinal analyses across study phases.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: automation vs nuance; weakness: options miss fallback for unusual events or expert arbitration when criteria fail.

levers.name: Capacity expansion governance with stage-gate decision framework
levers.consequences: Direct effect: ensures expansion aligns with validated data quality and safety metrics; downstream: potential delays if milestones are not met, and financial planning becomes more conservative.
options: Define stage-gate expansion triggers based on enrollment pace and data throughput before increasing bed count.
Pilot a controlled expansion of two additional suites after achieving validated data quality and incident-free operation for a defined period.
Develop a flexible budget plan that models expansion scenarios against grant milestones and regulatory approvals to minimize financial risk.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: growth versus safety and data quality; weakness: options miss explicit contingency planning for external funding or partner-driven expansion.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     - "Prioritizing speed over reliability carries hidden costs: none of the options address rollback complexity."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
